coredata.dc:title: Biomass based nanofiber membrane composite with xylan derived carbon dots for fluorescence detection nitrite in food real samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhiyuan
Zhang, Yingying
Feng, Xiuyuan
Cui, Xiaoci
Xiao, Huining
Yang, Anquan
Xie, Min
Xiong, Ranhua
Cheng, Weixia
Huang, Chaobo
coredata.dc:description: In our latest research endeavor, we are proud to present an innovative approach to the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) derived from the biomass xylan, which we have termed P-CDs. These P-CDs are meticulously integrated with a state-of-the-art biomass nanofiber membrane composed of polycaprolactone (PCL) and polylactic acid (PLA), resulting in the creation of a novel solid-state fluorescent sensor, designated as NFP-CDs. This cutting-edge sensor has been meticulously engineered for the highly sensitive and specific detection of nitrite ions (NO2 −), a critical parameter in various fields. The NFP-CDs sensor stands out for its user-friendly design, cost-effective production, and portable nature, making it an ideal choice for rapid and visible nitrite ion detection. It exhibits an extraordinary response time of less than 1 s, which is a testament to its high sensitivity. Furthermore, the sensor demonstrates exceptional selectivity and specificity, with a remarkably low detection threshold of 0.36 μM. This is achieved through a sophisticated dual detection mechanism that synergistically combines colorimetric and spectral analyses, ensuring accurate and reliable results. In addition to its impressive technical specifications, the NFP-CDs sensor has been rigorously tested and validated for its efficacy in detecting nitrite ions in real-world samples. These samples include a diverse range of food products such as rock sugar, preserved mustard, kimchi, and canned fish. The sensor has demonstrated a remarkable recovery rate, which varies from 99 % to 106 %, highlighting its potential for practical application in nitrite ion detection. This research not only offers a robust and effective strategy for the detection of nitrite ions but also carries profound implications for enhancing food safety and bolstering environmental monitoring efforts. The development of the NFP-CDs sensor represents a significant step forward in the field of sensor technology, providing a powerful tool for the detection of nitrite ions and contributing to the broader goals of public health and environmental stewardship.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylan
Carbon dots
Biomass
Fluorescence
Nanofibers membrane
NO2 − detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and optical property of NFP-CDs
Application of NFP-CDs for detection NO2 − in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and schematic diagram of xylan derived biomass CDs based nanofiber membranes. serves as solid state fluorescence sensor for NO2 − detection.

(A) Schematic diagram of P-CDs synthesis process. (B) TEM image of P-CDs. (C) Size distribution of P-CDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of P-CDs. (E) Fluorescence lifetime spectrum of P-CDs. (F) XPS spectrum of P-CDs. (G) High-resolution spectrum of C 1s. (H) High-resolution spectrum of O 1s. (I) High-resolution spectrum of Na 1s. (J) High-resolution spectrum of P 2p.
(A) Ultraviolet visible absorption spectrum of P-CDs. Inset: color of P-CDs under sunlight and Uv-vis lamp (365 nm). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of P-CDs.

(A) Schematic diagram of the preparation of NFP-CDs as fluorescence sensor. (B) TG analysis curve of NFP-CDs. (C) Stress-strain curve NFP-CDs. (D) Digital image of NFP-CDs under UV–vis light. (E) SEM image of NFP-CDs. (F) Size distribution of NFP-CDs. (G) Digital image of NFP-CDs + NO2 − under UV–vis light. (H) SEM image of NFP-CDs + NO2 −. (I) Size distribution of NFP-CDs + NO2 −.

(A) Fluorescence intensity change of NFP-CDs under various analytes (20 μM, 1: control, 2: K+, 3: Fe2+, 4: Fe3+, 5: Ag+, 6: Mg2+, 7: Zn2+, 8: Cr3+, 9: Al3+, 10: Pb2+,11: NO2 −,12: Cu2+,13: F−, 14: Cl−, 15: CN−, 16: HPO4 2−, 17: ClO−, 18: Hg2+, 19: SCN− and 20: HS−)(λ ex = 305 nm) and NFP-CDs + NO2 − + others analytes (50 μM, 1: control, 2: K+, 3: Fe2+, 4: Fe3+, 5: Ag+, 6: Mg2+, 7: Zn2+, 8: Cr3+, 9: Al3+, 10: Pb2+,11: NO2 −,12: Cu2+,13: F−, 14: Cl−, 15: CN−, 16: HPO4 2−, 17: ClO−, 18: Hg2+, 19: SCN− and 20: HS−). (B) Fluorescence titration curve. (C) Linear relationship of NFP-CDs under different concentration of NO2 −. (D) Time stability of NFP-CDs, NFP-CDs + NO2 −.
Biocompatibility and survival rate of zebrafish after cultured with NFP-CDs for 15 d.
(A) Schematic diagram of detecting NO2 − in food by using NFP-CDs. (B) Real images of NFP-CDs detection NO2 − captured by using iPhone 13 pro.
Comparison of the LOD of sensor with previously reported sensors.
Detection of NO2 − concentration in real food samples.

head.ce:title: Biomass based nanofiber membrane composite with xylan derived carbon dots for fluorescence detection nitrite in food real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent), Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (NFU), Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent)
Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent), Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (NFU), Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Children's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Children's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick E3B5A3, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang OSM Group Co., Ltd, Huzhou 313000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang OSM Group Co., Ltd, Huzhou 313000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In our latest research endeavor, we are proud to present an innovative approach to the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) derived from the biomass xylan, which we have termed P-CDs. These P-CDs are meticulously integrated with a state-of-the-art biomass nanofiber membrane composed of polycaprolactone (PCL) and polylactic acid (PLA), resulting in the creation of a novel solid-state fluorescent sensor, designated as NFP-CDs. This cutting-edge sensor has been meticulously engineered for the highly sensitive and specific detection of nitrite ions (NO2 −), a critical parameter in various fields. The NFP-CDs sensor stands out for its user-friendly design, cost-effective production, and portable nature, making it an ideal choice for rapid and visible nitrite ion detection. It exhibits an extraordinary response time of less than 1 s, which is a testament to its high sensitivity. Furthermore, the sensor demonstrates exceptional selectivity and specificity, with a remarkably low detection threshold of 0.36 μM. This is achieved through a sophisticated dual detection mechanism that synergistically combines colorimetric and spectral analyses, ensuring accurate and reliable results. In addition to its impressive technical specifications, the NFP-CDs sensor has been rigorously tested and validated for its efficacy in detecting nitrite ions in real-world samples. These samples include a diverse range of food products such as rock sugar, preserved mustard, kimchi, and canned fish. The sensor has demonstrated a remarkable recovery rate, which varies from 99 % to 106 %, highlighting its potential for practical application in nitrite ion detection. This research not only offers a robust and effective strategy for the detection of nitrite ions but also carries profound implications for enhancing food safety and bolstering environmental monitoring efforts. The development of the NFP-CDs sensor represents a significant step forward in the field of sensor technology, providing a powerful tool for the detection of nitrite ions and contributing to the broader goals of public health and environmental stewardship.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) serves as a common food additive with broad applications in various food processing industries, including pickled meat products, jams, dairy items, preserved meats, and others [1]. It functions as a color enhancer in meat products by reacting with myoglobin to produce nitroso myoglobin, which improves color, enhances flavor, and inhibits the growth of harmful bacteria such as Clostridium botulinum [2,3]. However, excessive levels of NO₂− in food can react with amino compounds, leading to the formation of toxic N-nitrosamines, which have been associated with deformities and cancer [4]. Additionally, NO₂− can bind with hemoglobin in human blood to form methemoglobin, a condition that reduces the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity and results in tissue hypoxia. Consequently, there is a critical need to develop reliable methods for detecting NO₂− in food samples to ensure food safety and reduce health risks associated with its excessive intake [5,6].

Currently, methods for detecting NO₂− include spectrophotometry, electrochemistry, chemiluminescence, chromatography, among others. Among these, fluorescence spectroscopy has gained considerable attention due to its simplicity and high sensitivity. A variety of fluorescent sensors, from small molecule probes to semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanoclusters, have been utilized for NO₂− detection [7,8]. However, the development of these probes often requires complex synthesis procedures and stringent purification processes, resulting in issues such as poor water solubility, high toxicity, and extended detection times. These drawbacks limit their applicability in environmental and biological settings. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop fluorescent sensors that are simple, sensitive, and biocompatible for effective NO₂− monitoring and detection.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have gained recognition as a promising and environmentally friendly alternative to petroleum-based chemical sensors for fluorescence detection. Due to their surface passivation and the presence of active organic groups, CDs exhibit tunable fluorescence emission, unique charge transfer properties, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity. As a result, they are widely used in various applications, including analytical detection, biological imaging, anti-counterfeiting, drug delivery, and photocatalysis [9–11]. Fluorescent sensing platforms utilizing CDs have demonstrated effectiveness in detecting metal ions, anions, and biomolecules. Current research is increasingly investigating the potential of fluorescent CDs for NO₂− detection, given their exceptional properties and versatility in various sensing applications [12,13]. Zhang et al., synthesized a blue fluorescent N-CNDs based on fluorescence quenching mechanism for NO2 − testing. Afterwards, literatures have successively reported CDs that can be used for direct, rapid, and simple detection of NO2 − [14]. Xiang et al., tried to extend the wavelength of NO2 − detection to the long wavelength region [6].

The preparation of biomass-derived CDs for fluorescence detection is increasingly prevalent due to their excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [15,16]. Biomass-derived CDs offer several advantages: their precursors are renewable, biodegradable, environmentally friendly and non-toxic [17,18]. Additionally, biomass-based CDs contain elements beyond carbon (C), hydrogen (H), and oxygen (O), making them an ideal carbon source for CD synthesis [19]. Among various biomass materials, xylan, a lignocellulosic resource, stands out for its non-toxic, renewable, biodegradable, and hydrophilic properties, offering significant potential for high-value applications [20–22]. he development of functional materials from xylan aligns with energy conservation, emission reduction, and sustainable development strategies, helping to mitigate the reliance on petrochemical resources. Xylan primarily comprises the elements C, H, and O [23–25], with a carbon content exceeding 40 %, ensuring the formation of the primary carbon skeleton structure of CDs during nucleation [26]. Furthermore, the oxygen-containing groups in xylan molecules create conditions for the formation of fluorescence emission potential wells in CDs, providing heteroatom doping sites that facilitate the regulation of fluorescence characteristics and emission wavelengths [27]. Thus, utilizing xylan as a green and renewable carbon resource to construct functional composite fluorescent CDs materials presents distinct advantages for NO₂− detection [28].

However, using CDs in solution for fluorescence detection poses challenges, such as the risk of leakage. Notably, biomass-based solid nanofiber membranes, prepared through electrospinning technology, offer significant advantages, including a large surface area, low porosity, and excellent biocompatibility [29–31]. Furthermore, electrospun nanofibers can enhance fluorescence sensors by enabling selective bonding between molecules and target substances via non-covalent or covalent interactions. This bonding can induce changes in molecular charge transfer or spatial structure, which are observed as shifts in molecular absorption spectra and color changes [32,33]. These properties significantly improve the sensitivity of colorimetric and spectral sensors [34,35]. Additionally, solid-state nanofiber membrane fluorescence sensors offer benefits such as portability, durability, and the capability for in situ real-time detection [36,37].

In this groundbreaking research, we have successfully synthesized CDs derived from biomass (P-CDs) that exhibit remarkable water solubility. This was achieved through a hydrothermal synthesis process, utilizing xylan as the carbon precursor and sodium phytic acid as a dopant. These P-CDs possess exceptional optical properties, making them ideal for sensing applications. To facilitate in-situ and real-time detection, we have ingeniously integrated these P-CDs into a biomass nanofiber membrane, which is a blend of polylactic acid (PLA) and polycaprolactone (PCL). The integration was accomplished using a straightforward physical in-situ impregnation technique, which is both efficient and cost-effective. The nanofiber membrane itself was fabricated using the advanced electrospinning technology, a method known for its ability to produce high-quality nanofibers with controlled morphology and alignment. The resulting solid-state fluorescence sensor, termed NFP-CDs, demonstrates a high degree of sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of NO₂− in real food samples. It operates on a ‘turn-off’ fluorescence mechanism, which provides a clear and visually discernible signal change, as depicted in Fig. 1 . This feature is particularly advantageous for naked-eye detection, eliminating the need for sophisticated instrumentation. Our innovative approach not only lays a solid foundation for the development of biomass-based fluorescent carbon dot nanofiber solid-state sensors but also significantly broadens the horizons for the detection of nitrite ions in real-world scenarios. This work paves the way for the creation of more sustainable and environmentally friendly sensing technologies, which are crucial for food safety monitoring and environmental analysis. Furthermore, the simplicity of the synthesis and integration processes, along with the robust performance of the NFP-CDs sensor, suggests that this technology has the potential for large-scale production and practical implementation. The versatility of the sensor platform could also be extended to detect other analytes of interest, thereby further enhancing its utility and impact in various fields.

Xylan (from birch, 95 %), sodium phytate (99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin reagent Co., LTD. Pickled vegetables and water are purchased from local supermarkets, zebrafish was purchased from Shanghai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Real soil samples were obtained from bamboo forests, small rivers, and ponds from campus, respectively. Polycaprolactone (PCL, average Mn 45,000), polylactic acid (PLA, Mw ∼ 60,000), hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP, 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin biochemical Co. Ltd., respectively.

Xylan was purified according to previous publication [38,39]. NaOH solution (20.0 mL, 2 %) was added into a beaker that containing xylan (2.0 g), then anhydrous ethanol (20.0 mL, 95 %) was added into above solution and magnetic stirred for 2.0 h. After xylan was completely precipitated and white flocculent was precipitated, the mixed solution was left to stand. Then, xylan was filtered through 0.45 μm filter membranes to obtain xylan powder with high purity.

Biomass based fluorescent CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using xylan and sodium phytate as raw materials. 0.2 g of xylan and 0.2 g of sodium phytate were dissolved in deionized water (20 mL). Above solution was transferred to hydrothermal reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (50 mL) and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. Centrifuge the obtained CDs solution at 10000 r/min for 10 mins and CDs solution was further purified through continuous dialysis (with a molecular weight of 500 Da retained by the dialysis bag) for 24 h to remove other small molecules. Dialyzed CDs solution was freeze dried to obtain powder, namely P-CDs.

By using in-situ electrospinning technology [40], a biomass-based nanofiber membrane was prepared served as fluorescence sensors. Electrospinning precursor solution mixtures including PLA (12 wt%) and PCL (12 wt%) were obtained by dissolving them in HFIP solution (10 mL) with overnight stirring [41,42]. The spinning precursors were added into a syringe (5 mL) and form nanofibers under the action of high voltage, and finally deposited on the collector with the 1.3 mL/h feed rate of the syringe pump. Besides, the distance between the needle tip and fly wheel collector (300 r/min) was 18 cm. A voltage of 18 kV was applied using a high voltage station. The as prepared nanofibers membrane was named as PCL/PLA. Then, by a simple physical dipping method, the PCL/PLA membrane was placed in a solution containing P-CDs and soaked in vacuum for 2 h at room temperature. Above membrane was taken out and washed with ultra-pure water to remove the unbound fluorescent P-CDs. The fluorescence nanofibers membrane was dried at 40 °C and named as NFP-CDs.

P-CDs (100 mg) powder was dissolved with distilled water (100 mL). And 1 mg/mL of P-CDs aqueous solution was obtained as sample solution [43]. Ultraviolet visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) was used to analysis the absorbance P-CDs solutions. Using deionized water as blank control, the scanning wavelength range was 200–700 nm and the scanning speed was 600 nm/min.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to analysis surface groups of samples. Samples were prepared by KBr laminating method with a scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1 and a resolution of 4.00 cm−1.

Using the fluorescence spectrometer (FS5) for analysis fluorescence properties of samples. Taking 1 mg/mL P-CDs aqueous solution as sample solution, the width of excitation and emission slit was set at 4 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of P-CDs were obtained with fixed excitation wavelength of 365 nm and emission wavelength range of 380 nm ∼ 800 nm. Using a fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, equipped with a solid-state fluorescence detection device) to detect the fluorescence characteristics of the fluorescent fiber membrane NFP-CDs. Specifically, nanofiber membrane was cutted into a certain size and bond it in a transparent colorimetric dish for corresponding testing.

Quantum yield (QY) was measured using a relatively classical method. Using quinine sulfate (QY = 55 % in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4) as the reference. The fluorescence quantum yield is 54 % at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The optical absorbance of sample aqueous solution and reference substance was measured. In order to reduce the reabsorption effect of fluorescent substances as much as possible, the concentrations of reference solution and sample solution were adjusted to make their absorbance values less than 0.05. Then, the fluorescence spectra of quinine sulfate and sample aqueous solution with known absorbance were tested. QY of the sample was calculated according following equation [27].
(2-1)
φ =
φ 0
×
A 0
I 0
×
I A
×
n 2
n 0 2
where φ , A, I, and n represent the QY, optical density, integral emission intensity, and refractive index (water = 1.33, ethanol = 1.36) of the sample, respectively, and
φ 0
, A 0, I 0, and n 0 represent the QY, optical density, integral emission intensity, and refractive index of quinine sulfate.

Preparation of NO2 − solution with concentrations of 1–50 μmol/L, 400 μL NO2 − solution of different concentrations were added into P-CDs solution (3 mL, 1 mg/mL), and PBS (2 mL, 0.1 mol/L, pH 3.0) was added. After shake for 30 mins, the mixture was evenly mixed and fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded. Three parallel experiments were conducted for each concentration gradient. The parameters were set as follows: the fixed excitation wavelength was 305 nm, the emission wavelength range was 380–800 nm, and both the excitation and emission slit widths were 4 nm.

The pickled mustard pulp, canned salted vegetables, and fish were each homogenized using a mechanical pulverization method. A 1.0 g portion of the homogenate was then weighed and placed into a 100.0 mL centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 40.0 mL of water. Ultrasonic extraction was performed for 30.0 mins, with agitation every 5.0 mins. The tubes were then immersed in a 75 °C water bath for 5.0 mins, cooled to room temperature, and transferred to 50.0 mL volumetric flasks. The solutions were filtered through filter paper and centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 15 mins. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the sample stock solution. Three replicates were prepared for each group. NFP-CDs were used for detection, and the average of the results was recorded. To assess the recovery of NO₂−, 100 μmol/L of NO₂− was added to the stock solution of preserved meat samples, and the recovery was determined using fluorescence titration spectroscopy.

In order to prepare green, fluorescent CDs materials, biomass-based CDs (P-CDs) materials was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using natural biomass resource xylan as starting material and sodium phytate as dopant, as shown in Fig. 2A. The morphologies of the synthesized CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2B, the size of P-CDs was uniform and approximately spherical, with good dispersion. Fig. 2C displayed that the size distribution of P-CDs ranged from 2.0 nm to 5.8 nm, and the average size was 3.5 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of P-CDs was shown in Fig. 2D. The peaks at 2925 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the saturated CH bond, and the peaks at 606 and 1398 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of the saturated CH bond. The peak at 1263 cm−1, 1043 cm−1 and 1169 cm−1 corresponded to the CO stretching vibration, the peak at 3406 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of -OH, and the peak at 1628 cm−1 corresponded to -OH deformation vibration, the peak of 1711 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of CO. Above results indicate that the prepared P-CDs contained with -OH group, which led to the good water solubility of prepared P-CDs. Fig. 2E showed that the fluorescence lifetime of P-CDs was 1.24 ns, which indicated good fluorescence property of P-CDs. The element content and surface composition of P-CDs were studied in detail by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2F, the XPS of P-CDs has four characteristic peaks at 285.01 eV, 532.19 eV, 1072 eV and 134. 6 eV, namely C 1s, O 1s, Na 1s and P 2p peaks respectively. Fig. 2G was a high-resolution spectrum of C1s, with three peaks of 284.81, 286.53 and 288.78 eV, corresponding to CO, C-C/C=C, and CO group, respectively. Fig. 2H was a high-resolution spectrum of O1s, with three peaks of 532.0 eV, 532.8 eV and 533.59 eV, corresponding to the CO, C-OH/C-O-C and CO functions, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of Na, P have a weak of Na 1s and P 2p peak, which indicated the successful doping of P element.

The optical properties of P-CDs were further examined, revealing a maximum absorption peak at 310 nm, as shown in Fig. 3A. Under sunlight, the P-CDs appeared brown, while under a UV–vis lamp (365 nm), they exhibited a blue fluorescence, indicating strong luminescence in aqueous solution (Fig. 3A inset). The fluorescence spectra of P-CDs demonstrated that as the excitation wavelength increased from 299 nm to 365 nm, the emission wavelength remained constant at 425 nm, suggesting that the emission wavelength of P-CDs is largely independent of the excitation wavelength. This independence implies that the size and surface state of the sp2 clusters within the P-CDs are consistent. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized P-CDs was calculated to be 35 %. The fluorescence selectivity of P-CDs was then investigated, as depicted in Fig. S1A. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs did not significantly increase or decrease upon the addition of various analytical substances. However, a notable enhancement in fluorescence intensity was observed after the addition of NO₂−, indicating that the carbon dots exhibit selectivity toward NO₂−. Furthermore, the detection of NO₂− by P-CDs was not affected by other substances, demonstrating the strong anti-interference capability of P-CDs for NO₂− detection. The effect of different concentrations of NO₂− on the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was also studied, as shown in Fig. S1B. The fluorescence titration curve revealed that the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs gradually increased with the rise in NO₂− concentration (0–400 μM). As shown in Fig. S1C and Fig. S1D, the relative fluorescence intensity of P-CDs and the concentration of NO2 − in range of 300–400 μM showed good linear relationship with F 0/Fmax = 0.86692 + 0.01218[NO2−], R2 = 0.9984. According to the formula 3S
d /K, the detection limit of the method was 0.47 μM. The above results show that the P-CDs has a good selectivity for NO2 − and has the potential for practical application.

The optical properties of P-CDs were further examined, revealing a maximum absorption peak at 310 nm, as shown in Fig. 3A. Under sunlight, the P-CDs appeared brown, while under a UV–vis lamp (365 nm), they exhibited a blue fluorescence, indicating strong luminescence in aqueous solution (Fig. 3A inset). The fluorescence spectra of P-CDs demonstrated that as the excitation wavelength increased from 299 nm to 365 nm, the emission wavelength remained constant at 425 nm, suggesting that the emission wavelength of P-CDs is largely independent of the excitation wavelength. This independence implies that the size and surface state of the sp2 clusters within the P-CDs are consistent. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized P-CDs was calculated to be 35 %. The fluorescence selectivity of P-CDs was then investigated, as depicted in Fig. S1A. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs did not significantly increase or decrease upon the addition of various analytical substances. However, a notable enhancement in fluorescence intensity was observed after the addition of NO₂−, indicating that the carbon dots exhibit selectivity toward NO₂−. Furthermore, the detection of NO₂− by P-CDs was not affected by other substances, demonstrating the strong anti-interference capability of P-CDs for NO₂− detection. The effect of different concentrations of NO₂− on the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was also studied, as shown in Fig. S1B. The fluorescence titration curve revealed that the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs gradually increased with the rise in NO₂− concentration (0–400 μM). As shown in Fig. S1C and Fig. S1D, the relative fluorescence intensity of P-CDs and the concentration of NO2 − in range of 300–400 μM showed good linear relationship with F 0/Fmax = 0.86692 + 0.01218[NO2−], R2 = 0.9984. According to the formula 3S
d /K, the detection limit of the method was 0.47 μM. The above results show that the P-CDs has a good selectivity for NO2 − and has the potential for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical property of NFP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: However, fluorescent CDs solutions are prone to leakage and can be affected by factors such as photothermal oxygen, leading to increased detection deviation. To achieve in-situ, real-time, and high-sensitivity detection of NO₂−, a solid-state fluorescence sensor was developed, as shown in Fig. 4A. This sensor was created by preparing solid-state nanofiber membranes with a large specific surface area and high porosity using electrospinning, which served as the substrate for P-CDs. The nanofiber membranes were then immersed in a P-CDs solution through a simple in-situ impregnation method to produce the solid-state fluorescence sensor, termed NFP-CDs.

The thermal stability and tensile properties of NFP-CDs were assessed. Thermal analysis (TGA) was used to evaluate the impact of functionalization on thermal stability and to confirm the formation of chemical bonds between PCL/PLA nanofibers and P-CDs, revealing differences in thermal behavior from the raw materials (Fig. 4B). Two distinct weight loss regions were observed between 50 °C and 800 °C in NFP-CDs. The weight loss between 50 °C and 350 °C was primarily due to the evaporation of physically adsorbed residual water, while the weight loss between 350 °C and 450 °C was associated with the degradation of the nanofiber structure. The stress-strain curve demonstrated that the NFP-CDs nanofiber membrane has good tensile properties (Fig. 4C). Fluorescence analysis of NFP-CDs showed that under UV light, the membrane appeared blue-purple, and the fluorescence color changed to bright blue upon the addition of NO₂−, indicating an enhanced fluorescence response to NO₂− (Fig. 4D and Fig. 4G). SEM images revealed that the NFP-CDs fibers were linear and distributed over a range of 0.4 μm to 0.7 μm, with an average diameter of 0.5 μm. After reacting with NO₂−, the nanofibers became curved, with an average diameter of 0.48 μm, demonstrating a change in micromorphology and confirming the sensor's selectivity and detection capability for NO₂−.

Then the fluorescence ability of NFP-CD for detection NO2 − was verified. When the NFP-CDs extract solution was mixed with different kinds of common analytes, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of NFP-CDs extract was significantly decreased when NO2 − was added, while the fluorescence intensity of other analytes was changed unobvious comparable to NFP-CDs. The results in Fig. 5A showed that NFP-CDs used as fluorescence sensor has excellent selectivity and response to NO2 −. In addition, the interference ability of other analytes on the detection behavior of NO2 − in NFP-CDs was also studied. It was found that the addition of other analytes did not interfere with the ability of NFP-CDs to detect NO2 −, which proved that NFP-CDs had a good anti-interference ability for detection NO2 − Then, different concentrations of NO2 − solution (0–500 μM) were added into NFP-CDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of NFP-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of NFP-CDs significantly decreased with the increase of NO2 − concentration. As shown in Fig. 5C, the relative fluorescence intensity of NFP-CDs and the concentration of NO2 − in the range of 10–500 μM showed good linear relationship with F 450nm = 402,087 + 3001[NO2 −], R2 = 0.9954. According to the formula 3S
d /K, the detection limit of the method was 0.36 μM, where S d was the standard of 10 blank samples, K was the slope of the fitted line. And compared with the reported reference, NFP-CDs depicted good detection limit for NO2 − (Table. 1 ). Besides, the change of NO2 − fluorescence intensity of NFP-CDs within 10 days was also tested, and the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the NFP-CDs and NFP-CDs + NO2 − did not change significantly with the increase of the test time shown in Fig. 5D and maintained good stability. The results showed that the NFP-CDs had good time stability for detection NO2 −.

Further, we verified the biocompatibility of NFP-CDs with living zebrafish. The results showed that zebrafish were still alive within co-cultured for 15 d, indicating that NFP-CDs has high biocompatibility, as shown in Fig. 6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NFP-CDs for detection NO2 − in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Widely recognized for its use in food preservation, NO₂− is commonly present in pickled and canned food products. In this study, we explored the efficacy of NFP-CDs fluorescence sensors in detecting NO₂− within real food samples, including rock sugar, preserved mustard, kimchi, and canned fish, as illustrated in Fig. 7A. Upon co-incubation of these food extracts with NFP-CDs, the fluorescence emitted by the NFP-CDs under a UV–vis lamp exhibited a dark blue hue. The introduction of an excess of exogenous NO₂− led to a bright blue fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 7B. These results highlight the potential of NFP-CDs for the detection of NO₂− in real food samples, indicating a promising direction for broader application.

In addition, the recovery rate of NO2 − in the actual food samples was 99 %–106 %, indicating the high accuracy and reliability of NFP-CDs for NO2 − detection in real samples, as shown in Table.2
.

In this research endeavor, we have crafted a novel class of fluorescent carbon dots (P-CDs) derived from biomass xylan, leveraging a straightforward and environmentally benign synthesis approach. P-CDs are characterized by their exceptional stability and sensitivity, making them ideal candidates for the development of advanced sensing technologies. By fusing these P-CDs with nanofiber membranes, which were meticulously produced via electrospinning—a technique renowned for its precision in fabricating ultrafine fibers—we have successfully engineered a solid-state fluorescence sensor. This innovative sensor, designated as NFP-CDs, is specifically designed for the detection of NO2 −, a critical parameter in various analytical fields. The NFP-CDs sensor stands out for its highly selective and sensitive real-time detection capabilities, offering a robust platform for monitoring NO2 − levels. During the detection process, the sensor exhibits notable colorimetric changes and amplified fluorescence responses, which not only provide a visual indication of the presence of NO2 − but also enable both qualitative and quantitative analysis.

NFP-CDs sensor has achieved a remarkable low detection limit of 0.36 μM, underscoring its high sensitivity. Moreover, it has demonstrated an impressive linear response in the range of 10 μM to 500 μM, which is a testament to its excellent sensitivity and selectivity for NO2 −. These characteristics are pivotal for accurate and reliable detection in a variety of settings. To further substantiate the practical utility of the NFP-CDs sensor, we conducted a series of tests on real-world samples, including rock sugar, preserved mustard, kimchi, and canned fish. The sensor performed admirably, with recovery rates that varied from 99 % to 106 %, indicating its effectiveness and reliability in real-life scenarios. These findings underscore the significant potential of NFP-CDs for NO2 − detection across a spectrum of applications, from food safety to environmental monitoring. The successful integration of biomass-derived P-CDs with nanofiber membranes, coupled with the sensor's outstanding performance, positions our NFP-CDs as a leading contender in the realm of solid-state fluorescence sensors. This research not only advances the field of sensing technology but also contributes to the broader goal of creating sustainable and eco-friendly analytical tools for the benefit of society and the environment.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
(A) Fluorescence selectivity and anti-interference ability of P-CDs (50 μM, 1: control, 2: K+, 3: Fe2+, 4: Fe3+, 5: Ag+, 6: Mg2+, 7: Zn2+, 8: Cr3+, 9: Al3+, 10: Pb2+,11: NO2 −,12: Cu2+,13: F−, 14: Cl−, 15: CN−, 16: HPO4 2−, 17: ClO−, 18: Hg2+, 19: SCN− and 20: HS−). (B) Fluorescence titration curve of P-CDs for detection NO2 − (0–400 μM). (C) Fluorescence titration scatter diagram of P-CDs for detection various concentration of NO2 −. (D) Fluorescence linear relationship of P-CDs for detection various concentration of NO2 − of P-CDs for detection various concentration of NO2 − (300–400 μM) (λ ex = 305 nm).
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.135693.

Zhiyuan Chen: Investigation. Yingying Zhang: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiuyuan Feng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Huining Xiao: Funding acquisition. Anquan Yang: Resources. Min Xie: Resources. Ranhua Xiong: Resources. Weixia Cheng: Resources. Chaobo Huang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We also thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22275094, 22275093) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_1159).
